text: The talk of {site} tonight, delivered by a patron who swears they were there: "{rumor}"

choices.text: Drink it in. History is free

outcomes.text: You commit it to memory, slightly wrong, continuing a proud oral tradition.
Lubricated, they add details: names, dates, a startling amount of slander. Someone slips you a coin for the entertainment.
The second round undoes them entirely. The story dissolves into weeping about a goose. You'll never know the ending.
The tavern turns on you. Historical revisionism is a bar-fight offense here.
An actual historian in the corner backs you up, citing sources. The teller buys YOU a drink out of shame.

text: Behind a collapsed wall, on a pedestal that has clearly eaten hands before: {relic}. It's real. It's here. The stories all end badly, but they do all mention it's worth a fortune.

outcomes.text: Your fingers close around it. Nothing bites. Somewhere, several factions just felt a disturbance in their ledgers.
The pedestal WAS a mimic. You take the relic anyway, mid-wrestle, because you have your priorities straight.

success.text: Three tripwires, a pressure plate, and a dart hole shaped like a disappointed face. You defeat them all with patience.

fail.text: You find the trap by triggering it. The dart's note reads 'told you.' You grab the relic while bleeding.

choices.text: Leave it. Some things should stay legendary

outcomes.text: You walk away. The pedestal seems almost disappointed. Your restraint feels valuable, which is good, because it's all you're leaving with.

text: A book stall run by a woman with ink-stained fingers and the eyes of someone who has read ALL the footnotes. 'Histories,' she says. 'Real ones. Six gold and I don't answer questions about chapter four.'

outcomes.text: The book is heavier than it looks. All the true ones are, she says, counting your coins.
She stares at you until you leave. Somehow you know three new things about the world anyway, none of them comfortable.

success.text: Four gold, final offer, and she throws in a bookmark shaped like a curse. Deal.

fail.text: She raises the price to seven, citing 'haggling tax.' You pay it. Chapter four remains unavailable.

text: A hooded figure at {site} slides you a drink. It is fizzing. Drinks here don't fizz.

outcomes.text: It's just soda. From space, apparently. Weirdly invigorating.
Poison. Classic. You spend an hour befriending the floor.
You can now taste colors. Purple is the worst one.
They vanish in a puff of embarrassment, leaving their coin purse.
Coward. Effective, living coward.

text: A stone golem sits at the corner table of {site}, arm up, palm open. The chalkboard reads: 'BEAT GRUNDLE. WIN 20 GOLD. NO REFUNDS ON BONES.'

success.text: Grundle's arm hits the table with a sound like a landslide. The bar goes silent, then erupts. Grundle nods, respectfully.

fail.text: Your arm makes a noise arms should not make.
You lose instantly, but with such style that someone buys you dinner.

choices.text: Bet on the next challenger instead

outcomes.text: The blacksmith beats Grundle on a technicality. Your bet pays out.
Grundle wins. Grundle always wins. The chalkboard was a warning, not a challenge.
Golems don't drink. Grundle holds the mug anyway, touched. You've made a very heavy friend.

text: Tonight's special at {site} is 'Stew.' No further details. The bowl is looking at you.

success.text: Delicious, restorative, and only mildly sentient. You feel great and vaguely guilty.

fail.text: The stew wins. You spend the night negotiating with your own stomach.

outcomes.text: The cook stares at you for a long time. 'Stew,' they say. You decide not to eat the stew. Wise.
The cook is so impressed someone finally asked that dinner is free.
The bread is fine. The bread is always fine. Boring, but fine.

text: A bard at {site} is singing a ballad about your life. The facts are wrong, the rhymes are worse, and the crowd loves it.

success.text: You bow. The crowd roars. Hats fill with coins, and one of the hats is yours.

fail.text: You bow. A chair leg is thrown. History will remember the ballad, not the truth.

outcomes.text: The bard incorporates your corrections into a new, crueler verse. It's catchy.
The bard hires you as a fact-checker on the spot. Easiest coin you've ever made.
You escape. Behind you, the ballad gains a verse about your mysterious vanishing. Cool points, technically.

text: A street prophet grabs your sleeve. 'You will die on a TUESDAY,' she hisses. She's selling anti-Tuesday charms, 10 gold each.

outcomes.text: It's a rock with 'NO' painted on it. You feel scammed and yet, somehow, safer.
The rock hums faintly. It might actually be doing something. Unsettling value for money.
She consults her notes. 'One of them,' she admits. Refreshing honesty. She gives you a discount rock for free.
She screams and sprints away. The crowd applauds. Someone hands you a celebratory pastry.
She smiles. That's the worst possible response. You spend the rest of the day looking over your shoulder.

text: Fingers, light as regret, brush your coin purse. The pickpocket is already three steps away, whistling.

success.text: You catch their wrist mid-whistle. They surrender your purse and, embarrassingly for them, someone else's too.

fail.text: You grab air. They grab your purse. The whistling fades into the crowd like an insult.

outcomes.text: The whole street tackles them out of civic enthusiasm. Your gold is returned, slightly dented.
Three other pickpockets use the distraction. Somewhere, a guild quota is met.
You nod. They nod. They keep 5 gold as a 'demonstration fee' and return the rest with a business card.

text: A guard blocks your path. 'Walking tax,' he says, inventing it. 'Five gold.' His halberd suggests this is not a negotiation.

success.text: You invent subsection 12-B, 'Exemptions for the Visibly Broke.' He writes it down, defeated by bureaucracy.

fail.text: He invents a fine for 'citing.' Now you owe seven.

outcomes.text: He pockets it and waves you through. At least the corruption is efficient here.

success.text: You vanish into an alley. The guard doesn't even give chase; armor is heavy and the day is long.

fail.text: You run directly into a second guard. There's a fee for that, too.

text: A vendor's stall displays gleaming junk: 'AUTHENTIC SKY-DEBRIS. FELL OFF A STAR-WAGON. NO REFUNDS.' One piece is blinking.

outcomes.text: It's a 'credit chip,' whatever that is. The vendor swears it's money somewhere. It does feel like money.
It blinks faster in your hand, then stops forever. You have purchased a dead rectangle.
It chirps 'RECALIBRATED' and zaps your brain pleasantly. You suddenly understand fractions.

choices.text: Ask where exactly it fell from

outcomes.text: The vendor points straight up and maintains eye contact. Technically unfalsifiable. You respect it.

choices.text: Report the stall to a guard

outcomes.text: The guard is the vendor's cousin. Everyone is disappointed in you, including, somehow, the merchandise.

text: The entrance door bears a carved sign: 'WELCOME. NO REFUNDS. MIND THE GAP.' There is, as far as you can see, no gap.

outcomes.text: There was a gap. You find it with your face. The dungeon's sense of humor is structural.
You step over where a gap would be, on a hunch. Somewhere, a trap sighs in disappointment.

success.text: The stick finds the gap, then a second gap, then a mimic pretending to be a third gap. You proceed safely, stickless.

fail.text: The stick was the mimic. It bites your hand and scampers off, offended.

outcomes.text: A voice from inside: 'It's open! Mind the gap!' Forewarned is forearmed. Whoever they are, you never find them.

text: A sword is wedged in a rib cage. The plaque reads: 'HERE LIES THE PREVIOUS OWNER OF THIS SWORD.' The sword looks excellent.

outcomes.text: Nothing happens. It's just a great sword and a very honest plaque.
The rib cage's original owner objects, briefly and skeletally. You keep the sword, but at a cost.
The sword whispers 'finally' in a voice like a satisfied tax auditor. You are now, apparently, in a partnership.

choices.text: Add a coin to the grave and move on

outcomes.text: The skeleton's jaw creaks into what might be a smile. Somewhere ahead, a trap quietly disarms itself.

success.text: '...ESTATE SALE SATURDAY.' You come back Saturday. The estate includes a modest bag of gold.

fail.text: The fine print is in Skeletal. You nod like you understood. The rib cage nods back. You leave quickly.

text: A goblin sits at a tiny desk before a bridge. 'Toll bridge,' he says. 'Five gold. I don't make the rules.' He gestures at a rock labeled RULES.

outcomes.text: He stamps your hand with a mushroom. 'Receipt.' The bridge is sturdy and the transaction weirdly professional.

success.text: You argue jurisdiction. The goblin consults the rock, loses the argument on your behalf, and waves you through, muttering about precedent.

fail.text: The rock wins. Somehow. There's a surcharge now for 'contempt of rock.'

success.text: Cold, wet, free. The goblin watches you the whole way, taking notes for a fee he'll invent later.

fail.text: The river is deeper, colder, and more opinionated than expected. You emerge downstream with less dignity and one fewer boot.

text: An altar to a forgotten god. The offering bowl holds three coins, a button, and a note: 'PLEASE. ANYTHING. I USED TO HAVE A WHOLE THING.'

outcomes.text: Warmth floods the room. A voice, rusty with disuse: 'A CUSTOMER.' You are blessed with the enthusiasm of the recently remembered.
The god overdoes the blessing, badly out of practice. Your hair will be golden for a month whether you like it or not.
Nothing happens. The god can't afford consequences right now.
A whisper follows you for the rest of the day: 'rude. RUDE.' Your luck feels thinner.

choices.text: Leave the button. Take nothing. Say something kind

outcomes.text: '...thank you,' says the dark, and means it. You leave feeling inexplicably taller.

text: The final chamber. An elderly lich sits at a desk buried in parchment. 'Adventurer,' it rasps, 'do you know ANYTHING about estate taxes? I've been dead for 300 years and they keep sending letters.'

success.text: Three hours later, you've found a deduction for 'phylactery depreciation.' The lich weeps dust and pays you from its hoard.

fail.text: You make it worse. The lich now owes back taxes in two kingdoms, one of which no longer exists. It curses you out of pure administrative grief.

success.text: The lich crumbles, almost relieved. Its final words: 'tell them... I'm... exempt.' The hoard is yours, minus filing fees.

fail.text: The lich hits you with a sheaf of parchment. It's the deadliest thing in the room and it knows it.

choices.text: Suggest it just stop opening the letters

outcomes.text: The lich stares at you. Three centuries of dread dissolve. 'I can... simply not?' It hands you gold with trembling claws. You've slain the real monster: obligation.

text: A mushroom the size of a stool clears its throat. 'I'll trade you a secret for a secret,' it says. 'That's the economy out here.'

outcomes.text: You whisper yours. It whispers back the location of a buried coin cache. Fungal intel is shockingly reliable.
Your secret is now known to the entire mycelial network. Every mushroom for miles is judging you.

choices.text: Ask why a mushroom needs secrets

outcomes.text: 'Leverage,' it says instantly. You have no follow-up questions. You have learned something anyway.
It tastes like betrayal and umami. The forest goes very quiet. You feel amazing and hunted.
It was ready for this. It is, in fact, mildly toxic out of spite alone.

text: A bear blocks the path, holding a piece of paper upside down. It growls, then, haltingly: 'You... read?' It appears to be a summons.

choices.text: Read the summons for the bear

success.text: The bear is being evicted from its own cave by a beaver holding company. You explain. The bear nods grimly and pays you in salmon and one shiny rock.

fail.text: You misread 'eviction' as 'invitation.' The bear attends the beaver gala. It does not go well. The bear blames you, physically.

outcomes.text: The bear watches you go, paper still upside down, somehow disappointed in the entire literate world.

success.text: You win on a technicality: beavers can't notarize. The forest pays you in prestige and buried acorn-gold.

fail.text: You lose to a beaver in a tiny waistcoat. The bear's look of betrayal will haunt you longer than the bruises.

text: A bush of plump berries, beside a hand-painted sign: 'DEFINITELY SAFE BERRIES.' The paint is fresh. The handwriting is nervous.

success.text: Sweet, harmless, delightful. The sign was telling the truth, which is somehow the biggest twist of your day.

fail.text: The sign lied. Your tongue goes numb and your shadow looks wrong for an hour.

outcomes.text: One berry's worth of poison is just a tingle. One berry's worth of delicious is still delicious. Science wins again, in miniature.

success.text: You find a goblin with purple-stained hands and zero remorse. He bribes you with real, verified rations to keep quiet.

fail.text: The trail leads in a circle back to the sign, which you'd swear now reads 'DEFINITELY SAFE BERRIES :)'.

text: You shout 'HELLO' into the dark. The echo answers 'HELLO,' then, after a pause, 'WHO TOLD YOU THAT NAME?'

success.text: Through careful questioning you determine the echo is guarding a stash it can't spend, being an echo. It trades coordinates for conversation.

fail.text: The echo answers every question with your own question, but smugger. You leave with a headache and existential doubts.

outcomes.text: 'THANK YOU,' says the echo, mollified. It guides you around a pit as a courtesy. Manners: still undefeated.
The echo repeats it to something bigger, deeper in the cave. You hear footsteps. You are suddenly very fast.
The echo laughs. An ancient tension breaks. It shares the cave gossip, including where the previous adventurer dropped their purse.

text: Something enormous is asleep across the passage. Its breathing sounds like a forge. A sign around its neck: 'DO NOT WAKE. SERIOUSLY. -MANAGEMENT.'

success.text: You ghost past it, stepping between snores. On the far side, someone has left a tip jar labeled 'FOR THE QUIET ONES.' You take a cut.

fail.text: You step on the one crunchy rock in the cave. The something opens one eye, decides you're not worth digesting, and swats you into a wall.

outcomes.text: Management was right. Management is always right. You survive on pure cardio.
It purrs. It PURRS. You have adopted something the size of a barn, emotionally if not logistically. It gifts you a shiny thing from its hoard.
You live to be a coward another day. The something snores approvingly.

text: A gang of goblins on rusty scooters circles you, engines buzzing like angry wasps. Their leader flips up her goggles: 'Tribute. Or race. Them's the roads.'

success.text: You win on a borrowed scooter with a wobbling wheel. The gang is furious and deeply impressed. They pay tribute to YOU, as is the law of the roads.

fail.text: You crash into the one bush on an otherwise empty plain. The gang laughs so hard two of them also crash. Tribute is waived for entertainment value, but the bush wants damages.

outcomes.text: The leader stamps your hand: road tax, paid. 'Valid till the next rain,' she says. It begins raining immediately.

success.text: There's a form. Of course there's a form. You're now an associate member with scooter privileges and a cut of today's tribute.

fail.text: 'We have standards,' says the goblin holding a scooter together with rope. The rejection stings more than it should.

text: A merchant sits beside a wagon with a shattered wheel, surrounded by crates labeled 'FRAGILE,' 'VERY FRAGILE,' and 'DO NOT ASK.'

success.text: The wheel goes on, the merchant beams, and you're paid from the DO NOT ASK crate. It's money. You don't ask.
Fixed! The merchant pays you in 'credits' - shiny rectangles from beyond the sky. 'Big somewhere,' they insist.

fail.text: The wagon rolls off the jack and onto your foot. The merchant apologizes on behalf of gravity.

outcomes.text: 'The first two are pottery.' You wait. The merchant stares through you. 'The pottery is also not to be asked about.' You move on, enriched only by mystery.

choices.text: Loot a crate while they're distracted

success.text: You palm something small from DO NOT ASK. It's warm. It hums. It's worth money to somebody, ideally somebody far away.

fail.text: The crate SHRIEKS. Whatever's inside does not appreciate being burgled. The merchant charges you an 'awakening fee.'

text: The barkeep of {site} slides you a tab as long as your arm. You ordered one drink. One item reads 'ambiance: 6 gold.'

success.text: You argue the ambiance was, at best, adequate. The barkeep, a professional, respects the negotiation and comps everything but the drink.

fail.text: The barkeep adds a 'dispute processing fee.' You pay it out of exhausted admiration.

outcomes.text: You pay in full with a flourish. Word spreads. By sundown, three taverns claim you drank there, and one has named a sandwich after you.

success.text: Out the window, over the fence, gone. Somewhere behind you the tab flutters to the floor like a defeated bird.

fail.text: The bouncer catches you by the collar mid-leap. The tab now includes 'window: 4 gold' and your dignity.

text: A small child in absurdly expensive clothes tugs your sleeve. 'I am LOST,' they announce, with the confidence of someone who has never once faced a consequence.

success.text: The manor guards initially arrest you, then the child vouches: 'This one is MY SERVANT.' The reward is generous. The title is not.

fail.text: The child directs you, turn by turn, to a candy stall, a puppet show, and a duck pond. By the time you find the manor, the reward has been spent on the journey. By the child.

choices.text: Deliver them to the city guard

outcomes.text: The guard sergeant recognizes the child, goes pale, and thanks you for making it someone else's problem. That's worth a small finder's fee from the guard's own purse.
The child watches you go, then declares, to the entire street, that you are their FAVORITE. You've never felt worse about a decision that cost nothing.

text: The final door opens onto a vault. Actual treasure, actually glittering. A plaque reads: 'CONGRATULATIONS. PLEASE REVIEW YOUR EXPERIENCE ON THE WAY OUT.'

outcomes.text: Coins, trinkets, a thing that might be jewelry or dentistry. A clean haul.
The floor was a scale. The exit fee is calculated by weight. Still profitable, barely.

choices.text: Take only what fits in one hand

success.text: Restraint! The vault's guardian, a tired spectral accountant, waves you through and rounds your haul UP for politeness.

fail.text: Your one hand cramps around the single heaviest object. You leave with it and a limp.

outcomes.text: You write 'atmospheric, good bones, gap unmarked' on the guest ledger. A small stipend of appreciation materializes. The dungeon has pride.

text: The last chamber holds no monster. It holds a janitor, mopping around the bones. 'Mind the wet bones,' he says. 'You here for the hoard? It's behind the mop closet. I don't make the rules but I do clean up after them.'

choices.text: Ask him to open the closet

success.text: He shrugs and unlocks it. 'Take the shiny ones. The cursed ones hum. You'll figure it out.' Solid professional advice.

fail.text: 'Closet's for staff.' He mops you gently but firmly toward the exit. You grab a souvenir from the lost-and-found on the way out.

choices.text: Fight him. He's clearly the boss

outcomes.text: He deflects your entire offense with the mop. Decades of technique. You wake up outside, neatly propped against a rock, with a pity coin in your palm.
You were right. He IS the boss. But so many years of mopping has left him mostly retired, and he yields the closet with grace and one devastating shoulder throw.
You mop. He talks. Three hours of dungeon gossip and a fair split of whatever rolls out from under the bones. Honest work.

text: The smithy of {site} rings with hammer-song. The smith looks up, appraises your gear with open pity, and gestures at the racks.

outcomes.text: The smith wipes their hands and starts the pitch.

success.text: The smith glows brighter than the forge. Word of your good taste spreads.

fail.text: You compliment the one piece their rival made. The temperature drops. The prices do not.

choices.text: Just warm your hands and leave

outcomes.text: The forge warms you to the bones. The smith nods. Some transactions are free.

text: A general store, shelves packed floor to ceiling. The sign says 'EVERYTHING MUST GO', and has said so, by the fading, for decades.

outcomes.text: The shopkeeper emerges from behind a pyramid of jars, delighted to have a customer who isn't a cat.
'Me,' says the shopkeeper, with the eyes of someone forty years into a going-out-of-business sale. You buy nothing but learn much.

text: A peddler's cart lurches to a stop beside you. The whole thing unfolds like a wooden flower into a shopfront. Impressive. Structurally alarming, but impressive.

outcomes.text: 'Road prices,' the peddler warns, meaning higher.
The demonstration re-folds the cart with the peddler inside. You help extract them. A discount is mentioned, emotionally, but a free sample is provided.
'Sky-metal hinges,' they whisper. 'Fell off a star-wagon.' Everything is sky-debris to salespeople now.
The cart refolds and trundles off, playing a tinny jingle that will live in your head for three days.

text: A cartographer slumps at the bar of {site}, surrounded by rolled maps and empty mugs. 'Treasure maps,' she says. 'Real ones. Eight gold. The fakes are eleven; better paper.'

outcomes.text: She picks one seemingly at random, kisses it for luck, and hands it over with the solemnity of a state funeral.

success.text: Five gold and you have to listen to the story of her divorce. Worth it. Mostly.

fail.text: She counter-haggles UP, citing 'emotional labor.' You pay nine before you understand what happened.

choices.text: Ask why the fakes cost more

outcomes.text: 'People trust expensive lies.' She taps her temple. You leave wiser and she orders another round on the strength of the lesson.

text: The corner table of {site} has gone quiet. That's {npc} - THE {npc}, {npc_trait} {npc_prof} of {npc_faction} - nursing a drink and a reputation.

choices.text: Buy them a drink and ask for the true story

outcomes.text: Two drinks in, the truth comes out: it was luck, mostly, and the ballads left out the running away. You feel better about your own odds.
They tell the story with full theatrical staging, using your drink as a prop. It's spent by the end. So are you. Magnificent.

success.text: You win. The tavern loses its mind. {npc} buys the room a round and announces your name like a herald. This will be a ballad.

fail.text: Your arm is a footnote in their legend now. They're gracious about it, which is worse.

choices.text: Pick their pocket. Legends carry legendary coin

success.text: You lift a purse heavy with story-money. You will never tell anyone how easy it was, because no one would believe you.

fail.text: They catch your wrist without turning around. '{npc_trait},' they say, as if reading your file. The bouncer provides the rest of the lesson.

text: A stranger at {site} is holding a portrait and a grudge. 'My grandparent, {gone}. Died in year {gone_year}. And do you know what the histories call it? A FOOTNOTE.'

outcomes.text: You listen to the whole family history. All of it. The stranger, unburdened, pays for your dinner and calls you 'the only decent soul left.'

choices.text: Claim you've heard great things about {gone}

success.text: You improvise three flattering anecdotes on the spot. The stranger weeps, then insists on giving you a family heirloom over your polite objections.

fail.text: You get the year wrong, the deeds wrong, and, impressively, the name wrong, mid-sentence. The portrait gets involved. Frames hurt.

choices.text: Point out that footnotes are still history

outcomes.text: The stranger blinks. 'Footnotes... are still history.' A tension generations deep dissolves over one round of drinks, which they buy.
Wrong crowd for nuance. You wear the drink they bought.

text: A recruiter at a folding table, banner overhead, quill ready. 'Sign here and be part of something. Benefits include belonging, a sash, and enemies you didn't choose.'

outcomes.text: You're in. There's a stipend, a sash, and a meeting schedule you will never fully escape.
The fine print includes dues. You've paid to join something you don't understand, which the recruiter calls 'tradition.'

success.text: You dismantle the pitch so thoroughly the recruiter takes the afternoon off to think. Onlookers applaud. One tips you.

fail.text: You lose a debate to a pamphlet. The pamphlet is well-written. You buy the pamphlet.

outcomes.text: The pamphlet is 90% sash diagrams. You feel you understand the organization completely now.

text: The city fountain. Locals swear it grants wishes. The bottom glitters with the copper of a thousand optimists.

outcomes.text: Nothing happens, but the plink is satisfying and hope is technically renewable.
The fountain gurgles approvingly. You feel measurably luckier, which should be impossible, and yet.
The fountain spits your coin back with interest. Do not question the fountain's accounting.

choices.text: Wade in and collect the coins

success.text: A swift, shameless harvest. You leave wet, richer, and cursed only by onlookers, which doesn't count.

fail.text: The guard was watching. The fine is itemized: theft of wishes, per wish.

outcomes.text: It's fountain water. It tastes of copper and other people's dreams. You feel briefly connected to everything, then mostly to your stomach.

text: A street magician is doing card tricks. The cards are floating. Actual levitation for copper tips, while three blocks away the guild sells the same magic for a fortune.

success.text: Threads of enchanted spider-silk. You catch the magician's eye; they wink and cut you in for keeping quiet.

fail.text: You stare so hard you volunteer yourself by accident. Your coin purse is now an egg. The crowd loves it. The magician cannot change it back.

outcomes.text: The magician makes your tip vanish, then makes a rose appear behind your ear. Net loss, gross delight.
The magician makes your eyebrows swap places for an hour. The crowd sides with the magician. So do your eyebrows.
Your heckle is so precise the magician hires you as a plant. Two shows later you've earned honest dishonest wages.

text: A dice game in the back of {site}. The stakes are five gold, the dice are 'house dice,' and the dealer's smile has too many teeth in it.

outcomes.text: The dice like you tonight. The dealer's smile loses a tooth of confidence.
The house dice do house things. You watch your coins join their family behind the counter.

success.text: Your palm is quicker than their loaded pips. You win with THEIR crooked dice, which every code of honor agrees is beautiful.

fail.text: Caught. The house takes your stake plus a 'cheater's tithe,' and everyone gets to watch.

outcomes.text: You clock the loaded dice, the sleeve, and the mirror in the lamp. An education, free of charge.

text: A hut on impressively muscular chicken legs settles down in front of you, blocking the path with intent. A witch leans out. 'Well? I don't stop for just anyone. The hut liked you.'

outcomes.text: Herbs, chanting, a soup that tastes like being twelve again. You leave whole and smelling faintly of thyme.
The witch and the hut exchange a look. 'Corn,' says the witch, in the exact tone of a lie. The hut licks its doorstep. You've learned something you can't unlearn.

success.text: The hut does a small, devastating curtsy. The witch, disarmed, gives you a jar of something restorative 'for the road, and because it flexed.'

fail.text: The hut takes it wrong and does a warning stomp. The resulting mud wave is personal.

text: A perfect circle of mushrooms in a clearing. Every childhood story you know screams in unison. The circle smells like rain and bad decisions.

outcomes.text: The world hiccups. You are somewhere else in the forest, three hours older, holding a flower you don't remember picking. Fair trade.
Applause from nowhere. Something appreciated your confidence and left a tip.
You are ejected from the circle by consensus. The bruise is shaped like disapproval.

choices.text: Leave an offering at the edge

outcomes.text: The coins sink into the moss with a sound like a purr. Your path out of the forest is inexplicably shorter and all downhill.
You monster. Delicious, delicious monster. The forest disapproves; your stomach files a dissent.
The mushrooms scream. Softly, but they scream. You stop immediately and pretend it never happened. The forest pretends with you.

text: A wizard stands at the roadside, thumb out, beard tucked into his belt for travel. His sign reads 'ANYWHERE BUT HERE. WILL ENCHANT FOR RIDES.'

outcomes.text: He rides side-saddle with tremendous dignity and enchants your scooter in payment. It now refuses to tip over and hums show tunes.
Two miles of wizard small talk, which is regular small talk but about portents. He teaches you a cantrip for keeping boots dry. Life-changing, honestly.
He talks the entire way about his ex-apprentice. THE ENTIRE WAY. At the crossroads he pays you for the therapy in coin, as is proper.
He looks back down the road. Far away, faintly, something honks. 'Geese,' he says. 'A grudge. Don't ask more.' You don't. The honking continues, patient.

text: A gleaming pillar of sky-metal blocks the road. It projects glowing text: 'TOLL: 5 CREDITS. CASH NOT ACCEPTED. HAVE A PRODUCTIVE CYCLE.' It has clearly been here since before anyone knew what credits were.

outcomes.text: You wave the blinking chip. The pillar chimes, thanks you for your 'loyalty,' and awards points you can never spend.

success.text: You convince the pillar you're a maintenance worker. It apologizes, opens, and pays YOU a service fee from its dusty internal purse.

fail.text: The pillar declares an 'unauthorized access event' and tases you politely. It then offers a customer satisfaction survey.

choices.text: Walk around it through the brush

outcomes.text: The pillar watches you the whole way, projecting the word 'NOTED.' The brush is full of thorns and the moral high ground.

text: The ceiling is a galaxy of glow worms, blue-green constellations rearranging slowly. It is genuinely, unfairly beautiful for a hole in the ground.

choices.text: Harvest a few for the glow

success.text: You bottle a soft constellation. Alchemists pay well for portable starlight.

fail.text: The worms coordinate. Every light goes out at once, and in the black something drips meaningfully. You leave with wet boots and an apology.

outcomes.text: You lie on a flat rock and watch the ceiling drift. Whatever was wound tight in you unwinds a notch. The worms neither know nor care. Perfect.
It tastes like mint and poor judgment. Your tongue glows for a day. Taverns will love you tonight.
Science reports: no. Your stomach glows faintly, visibly, through you. Alarming for everyone.

text: An underground lake, black and still as held breath. Something in the middle of it is faintly, patiently, ringing. Like a bell. Like an invitation.

success.text: The bell is a sunken shrine's. Around its clapper, offerings of centuries: coins, rings, one impressively preserved cheese. You tithe yourself a share.

fail.text: The cold gets a vote and vetoes you halfway. You crawl out shivering. The ringing sounds distinctly amused now.

outcomes.text: The fish are pale, eyeless, and delicious-looking in a way you choose to trust. Provisions acquired.
You catch a boot. Inside the boot: coins. Inside the coins: no further mysteries, thankfully. Just coins.

choices.text: Skip a stone at the ringing

outcomes.text: Four skips, then silence. Total silence. The ringing does not resume. You feel you have won something and also ended something. The cave feels emptier on the way out.

text: Dusk. A good sheltered spot presents itself: flat stone, dry wood, the kind of quiet that makes camp sound like wisdom.

outcomes.text: Fire, food, and the deep sleep of someone who packed the right equipment. Morning finds you new.

success.text: Cold ground, warm cloak, decent stars. You wake stiff but restored.

fail.text: It rains. The shelter that looked wise redirects the water directly onto you, with craftsmanship.

outcomes.text: You make good time in the dark and feel like a legend. An exhausted legend.
Night travel introduces you to a ditch. Personally.

text: The final chamber is... a gift shop. Shelves of branded merchandise: tiny plush mimics, 'I SURVIVED {site}' mugs, novelty curse scrolls. A skeleton in a lanyard waves. 'Exit's through here. Everyone exits through here.'

outcomes.text: The plush mimic is soft, judgmental, and possibly load-bearing to your happiness now.
The mug is real. The curse scroll was also, it turns out, real. Complimentary with purchase.

choices.text: Ask what happened to the actual boss

outcomes.text: 'Franchised,' says the skeleton, adjusting its lanyard. 'The margins on terror are awful. Retail is forever.' It comps you a keychain for asking.

success.text: You palm a snow globe of the dungeon itself. Inside it, a tiny you palms a tinier snow globe. Best not to think about it.

fail.text: The plush mimics were real mimics. Of course they were. Store security is impeccable and has teeth.

text: The public bathhouse: steam, echoes, and the total collapse of social rank. Somewhere in the fog a voice holds court: "{rumor}"

outcomes.text: Two hours of heat, gossip, and mineral water. You emerge pruned, informed, and at peace.

choices.text: Correct the storyteller from inside the fog

outcomes.text: An anonymous debate erupts across the steam. You win. Someone unseen slides you a respect pastry.
The fog turns hostile. You are asked to leave by consensus splash.

choices.text: Check the changing room for unattended purses

success.text: A professional sweep. You even leave the sentimental lockets, because you're a thief, not a monster.

fail.text: The attendant was a retired adventurer. The towel-whip is a martial art here.

text: A crowd has gathered in the square. On the scaffold, a man is being sentenced for 'aggravated mime-craft.' The crowd's mood is somewhere between festival and refund.

success.text: Your speech about proportionality moves the magistrate to tears and the sentence to community theater. The mime thanks you, silently but at length.

fail.text: The crowd boos you in unison. The mime, professionally, mimes booing you as well. Even the condemned have standards.

success.text: Executions are a pickpocket's harvest festival. You tithe yourself generously from six pockets.

fail.text: Your target was the executioner, off-duty. There's a fine. He deducts it directly, by handshake.

outcomes.text: You walk away from the roar of the square. Somewhere behind you, an invisible box gets its final performance. Your conscience travels light today.

text: A currency exchange run by a woman with an abacus, a sky-metal calculator, and visible contempt for both. The board lists today's rate for 'star money' in chalk, already crossed out twice.

outcomes.text: She counts out coins with the weariness of someone who's seen currencies die. Fair rate, all things considered.
The rate improved while you stood there. She honors the chalk, cursing softly. Windfall.
'Investing in the sky economy,' she says, deadpan, sliding the blinking chips across. 'Bold.'

success.text: You and she talk monetary theory for an hour. Her conclusion: 'turnips, eventually.' You leave smarter and vaguely alarmed.

fail.text: She answers entirely in exchange-rate jargon. You nod along and accidentally agree to a small transaction fee for the lesson.

text: It's open-verse night at {site}. The bard's lute is passed from table to table like a dare. It is now in front of you. The room waits.

success.text: You bring the house down with a ballad about a goblin toll collector and his rock. Coins rain. The barkeep declares your tab 'historical fiction.'

fail.text: You forget the words to a song you invented on the spot. The silence has texture. The lute is gently confiscated.

choices.text: Play something instrumental. No words, no risk

outcomes.text: Wordless and haunting. Nobody can mock lyrics that don't exist. A tactician's performance, modestly tipped.

choices.text: Pass the lute and buy the next performer a drink

outcomes.text: The next performer, emboldened by your drink, is magnificent. You are remembered as part of the story. Adjacent glory still counts.

text: Behind the bar of {site}, a keg is chained shut. Not locked. CHAINED. The barkeep follows your gaze. 'House special. Waiver's under the till.'

choices.text: Sign the waiver. Drink the special

success.text: It tastes like lightning that's been through something. You remain standing. The bar applauds. The barkeep frames the waiver, unneeded.

fail.text: You wake up two hours later under a table you don't recognize, holding a certificate that says 'PARTICIPANT.'

outcomes.text: Clause four covers 'temporary translucency.' Clause nine is written in a language that hurts. You learn everything you need to know without drinking anything.
The barkeep leans in: 'The last three people who asked are the reason for the chain.' You order water. The water is excellent.

text: A gelatinous cube fills the corridor, drifting slowly, absorbing bones and debris. A sign taped to it reads: 'CLEANING IN PROGRESS. PLEASE USE OTHER HALL.' There is no other hall.

outcomes.text: Twenty minutes of respectful silence. The cube passes, leaving the corridor immaculate. You could eat off this floor. Something clearly has.
While waiting, you notice a coin purse suspended in the cube, mid-digestion. You fish it out with a stick and quick hands. Finders keepers; the cube doesn't argue.

success.text: You limbo along the wall, inches from digestion. The cube ripples, possibly in appreciation of your form.

fail.text: It got your sleeve. Just the sleeve. The dissolving noise will feature in your dreams, but the arm is fine.

outcomes.text: The cube stops. Quivers. In eight hundred years, no one has ever. It extrudes a small, perfectly cleaned trinket from its interior: a tip, or a gift, or a hostage. You take it either way.

text: A door with four dials and an inscription: 'SPEAK THE ANSWER: WHAT WALKS ON FOUR LEGS AT DAWN, TWO AT NOON, AND THREE AT DUSK?' Below, scratched by a previous visitor: 'ITS NOT MAN. WE TRIED MAN.'

success.text: 'A dog. A dog that learned to beg, then hurt its paw.' The door considers this, decides it respects you, and opens. Inside: the previous riddler's abandoned supplies.

fail.text: You say 'man' anyway, with confidence. The door administers a corrective jolt. The scratched warning gains a tally mark, courtesy of your elbow.

outcomes.text: It was never locked. The riddle was decorative. Somewhere, an architect is still laughing.
The handle was the trap. The riddle was the distraction. Your hand is the lesson.
You pose one about a merchant, a wagon, and three crates labeled DO NOT ASK. The door thinks for a full minute, then opens out of intellectual courtesy. Doors get bored too.

text: You interrupt a circle of chests, barrels, and one apologetic bookshelf arranged in a ring. A banner droops overhead: 'DISGUISE-DEPENDENT PREDATORS ANONYMOUS. ALL FORMS WELCOME.'

outcomes.text: A barrel shares its fear of being opened 'for the wrong reasons.' You cry a little. Everyone pretends not to notice, which for mimics is deeply on-brand. They gift you a member's token.

choices.text: Ask which of them is the real treasure chest

outcomes.text: A hurt silence. 'We're ALL real,' says the bookshelf, quivering. You have set the group back weeks and are asked to leave.
One chest, new to the group and weak of will, cracks open to prove itself. Gold spills. The group sighs. Relapse.

choices.text: Share your own struggles with pretending

outcomes.text: You talk about smiling through tavern ballads about your life. The circle is moved. A chest dabs its lid. You leave lighter, with the group's blessing and a snack for the road.

text: A goblin scooter lies crashed against a stump, wheels still spinning. Its rider is gone. A note pinned to the seat: 'BACK IN FIVE MINUTES. DO NOT TOUCH. -DEATHWHEEL THE UNSTOPPABLE'. The note is weathered by months of rain.

success.text: Bent forks, loose chain, mysterious lever that does nothing. An hour's work. Deathwheel's loss is your commute.

fail.text: The mysterious lever did something after all. The scooter, briefly, flies. Then teaches you why it shouldn't.

outcomes.text: You wait ten, out of courtesy. Deathwheel does not return. Deathwheel has not returned for some time, the moss implies. You leave a small stone as a memorial and feel briefly, unaccountably noble.
Chain, bell, one good wheel, and a saddlebag containing Deathwheel's emergency stash. The forest reclaims the rest.

text: A beehive the size of a wardrobe hums in the crook of an oak. The bees seem organized. Suspiciously organized. There are little guard rotations.

success.text: In, out, one sting as a processing fee. The comb is heavy and golden and worth every welt.

fail.text: The guard rotation was for exactly this. The swarm escorts you a full quarter mile with prejudice.

choices.text: Leave an offering of your rations

outcomes.text: The bees inspect the trade, confer, and approve. Comb is lowered to you on a strand of wax like a tiny crane delivery. Commerce.
An hour of watching reveals shift changes, a dance-based bureaucracy, and what is unmistakably a customs checkpoint. You take notes. Scholars will call you mad. Scholars weren't here.

text: A chamber of crystals, each humming a different note. Together they almost make a chord. Almost. One crystal is flat, and the whole cave seems embarrassed about it.

success.text: A quarter-turn, a held breath, and the chord LOCKS. The harmonic wave that rolls through the cave shakes loose centuries of dropped coins from the ceiling cracks.

fail.text: You make it worse. The dissonance is now weaponized. Every crystal turns its hum on you until you leave, ears ringing with disapproval.

outcomes.text: You supply the missing note yourself. For one shining minute, you are part of a geological choir. Nothing about your life feels small right now.
You are also flat. The cave now has two problems. The crystals dim pointedly.

choices.text: Chip off a crystal to sell

outcomes.text: The note dies in your hand; loose crystals don't sing. It's still pretty, and pretty sells, but you'll remember the silence at odd moments for years.

text: A funeral procession fills the road - for {gone}, dead these many years, whose remains are only now being moved to a fancier grave. The mourners are professionals; the grief is rented; the traffic is real.

choices.text: Join the procession for a while

outcomes.text: You walk a mile behind the casket of a stranger. The professional mourners teach you two harmonies and a surprising amount about {gone}. Attendance includes a wake-lunch.
You thread the gap between casket and clergy at a jog. Gasps. Someone faints professionally. You clear it, pursued only by reputation.
You misjudge the pallbearers' pace. The collision is slow, ceremonial, and witnessed by everyone. {gone}'s remains are fine. Your standing is not.

choices.text: Ask why the upgrade after all these years

success.text: A mourner breaks character: the estate finally settled, and the lawyers took everything but the grave budget. You get the whole saga and a tip for listening to THEIR grief about it.

fail.text: 'Ghoul,' hisses the head mourner, misunderstanding completely. You are moved along by six umbrellas.

text: A crossroads signpost with one arm too many. Three point to real places. The fourth, freshly painted, reads 'DEFINITELY NO TREASURE THIS WAY (BORING) (DO NOT)'. The handwriting is familiar. Nervous.

outcomes.text: The path winds into deep green quiet. Someone worked hard to keep people away from here.

choices.text: Follow a real sign like a sensible person

outcomes.text: You take the boring road. It is boring. It is also entirely free of whatever that other path was about, and some days that's the whole victory.
You paint 'FREE SOUP AHEAD' pointing into a swamp. It's cruel. It's hilarious. Somewhere down the line, a berry-goblin nods in professional respect.

text: The path opens into a hidden glade: a ring of wildflowers, a stone table, and a strongbox half-swallowed by moss. Whoever painted the sign lived here. Judging by the tin cups - two of everything - they didn't live alone. Judging by the dust, that was a long time ago.

outcomes.text: Coins, a folded map, and a note: 'If you found this, we didn't come back. Spend it loudly.' You intend to honor that.
The box holds coins and a stack of letters you have no right to read. You read one. Just one. Then you close the box, take the coins, and leave the words for the moss.

choices.text: Rest in the glade without touching anything

outcomes.text: You eat lunch at a dead stranger's stone table and feel, oddly, like a guest rather than an intruder. The glade is good at that. You leave it exactly as found.

choices.text: Set the fake signpost pointing here on fire on your way out

outcomes.text: Let the glade keep itself. Some secrets deserve better security than nervous handwriting. The flames feel like a favor to someone who can't say thanks.
